title: Rag rug from T-shirt strips (rectangular)
subtitle: A 60 x 90 cm locker-hooked rag rug on hessian backing, worked in T-shirt strip yarn
excerpt: A 60 x 90 cm rag rug worked in T-shirt strip yarn on hessian backing. Strips are cut at 18 to 20 mm wide from old cotton T-shirts and pulled through the hessian with a rug hook in a simple striped or geometric pattern. Edges are bound with rug binding tape mitred at the corners. One adult T-shirt yields roughly 7 m of 18 mm strip; the finished rug uses approximately 30 T-shirts.
sourceNotes: The domestic rag rug is a longstanding British and North American recycling tradition: worn clothing and household linens cut into strips and worked through a backing. Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910), Internet Archive, covers strip preparation for rag rugs in passing. The locker-hook technique as applied to hessian with T-shirt yarn follows standard modern UK guild instruction; no single named modern author is the primary source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A coarse, open-weave jute cloth used as the foundation fabric for hooked and locker-hooked rugs. The open weave allows a rug hook or punch needle to pass through cleanly on every stroke. Standard rug-grade hessian runs at 10 holes per 5 cm; finer hessian at 8 holes per 5 cm gives a denser pile.

The cut width of fabric strips used in rag-rug making, which determines the pile height and density of the finished rug. For T-shirt jersey, 18 to 20 mm is the standard working width on coarse-weave hessian: the strip fits the backing holes cleanly and the pile sits at roughly 12 mm when looped. Narrower strips produce a flatter, finer pile; wider strips produce a higher pile but may not pass through the backing cleanly.

A heavy cotton or linen tape sewn around the perimeter of a finished rug to cover the cut edge of the backing fabric and reinforce the outermost rows of pile. Standard rug binding tape is 25 to 50 mm wide and sold by the metre. At corners, the tape is mitred to lie flat without bunching.

A rug-making technique in which fabric strips are pulled through a backing in loops using a hooked tool, then locked in place by threading a locking cord or the strip tail back through the loop on the reverse face. The locking action prevents the loops pulling free from the back. On T-shirt strip work, the strip itself serves as both the pile and the locking element.

content.text: This rug is made from T-shirt cotton pulled through a
hessian backing
with a rug hook, then bound at the edges with rug binding tape. The finished size is 60 x 90 cm. The pile is formed from continuous jersey strips cut at
18 to 20 mm strip width
; at that width, one adult T-shirt yields roughly 7 m of strip when cut in a continuous spiral, and the rug uses approximately 30 T-shirts in total. The
locker-hook technique
keeps each loop secured through the backing, so the pile resists the pulling that plain hooking can allow.

The design can be horizontal stripes in two or three colours, or a stepped border around a plain centre. Sort shirts into colour groups before cutting so you can plan stripe widths without stopping mid-row.
What you need

items.name: Old cotton T-shirts (approximately 30, in 2 to 3 colours)
Hessian backing, 10 holes per 5 cm, rug grade
items.qty: 75 x 110 cm (allows 7.5 cm overhang on all sides)

items.name: Permanent marker pen or fabric chalk
Kitchen scales (to estimate strip quantity by weight)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the backing

Lay the hessian flat and fold the raw edges back by 2.5 cm all around, creasing firmly. The fold prevents fraying during work and gives a clean edge to bind later. Fix the hessian to the rug frame, stretching it taut in both directions so the weave sits square. Use the gripper strips on the frame's inner edges to hold the fold, not the body of the cloth.

Mark the 60 x 90 cm rug area centred on the hessian with a permanent marker, following the thread lines of the weave so the design stays on-grain. Mark a 2 cm border line inside that outer rectangle: this is where the edge rows of pile will stop, leaving space for the binding tape. If you are working a geometric pattern, draw the full design on the hessian now. For stripes, mark only the colour-change lines.
Step 2: Prepare the T-shirt strips

Lay each T-shirt flat and cut off the hem and seams. Cut the remaining tube of fabric into a continuous spiral: start at one corner of the open tube and cut across the body at 18 to 20 mm width, turning at each edge to continue the strip without breaking it. A consistent width matters: strips that vary from 15 to 25 mm will produce an uneven pile height. One medium adult T-shirt cut at 18 mm yields roughly 7 m of strip; a large shirt can yield 9 to 10 m.

Wind each colour's strips into loose balls and weigh them. The finished 60 x 90 cm rug uses approximately 210 m of strip in total, which weighs roughly 700 to 800 g depending on the fabric weight of the T-shirts. Heavier cotton gives a denser pile. Sort your balls by colour and set them in the sequence you will use them.
Step 3: Work the pile from the outside in

Begin at the outermost row of the design, just inside the 2 cm border line. Hold the strip underneath the hessian with the non-working hand. Push the rug hook down through a hole in the hessian, catch the strip from below, and pull a loop up through the hole to a height of approximately 12 mm. Move one hole along and repeat. The loops should stand upright and even; if a loop collapses sideways, the strip is too wide for the weave and needs trimming.

Work row by row, moving from the outside of the design toward the centre. Working outside-in keeps the tension more even: the outermost rows, which are worked first, can be re-tensioned slightly as the inner rows fill the backing. Leave one hole empty between each loop row to allow the pile to splay slightly and cover the backing.

To join a new strip, leave the tail of the old strip standing up through the hessian at 12 mm, and bring the tail of the new strip up through the same hole or the adjacent hole. Both tails become part of the pile surface and are trimmed flush with the surrounding loops at the end. Do not tie strips together on the underside: the knot creates a bump on the back face and prevents the rug from lying flat.
Step 4: Secure the edge rows and remove from the frame

When the last row of pile is complete, check that the outermost loops are as firmly seated as the interior rows. Run a row of stitching by hand through the hessian backing, just outside the pile area, using strong linen thread and the blunt needle. This running stitch holds the edge fold in place and prevents the backing fraying when the rug is removed from the frame. Remove the rug from the frame by lifting it off the gripper strips carefully, working along one edge at a time.
Step 5: Trim the pile and check the surface

Lay the rug pile-face up and look across the surface at a shallow angle. Any loops standing noticeably higher than their neighbours will be visible as raised tufts. Trim these flush with sharp scissors. Strip tails at colour-change points should also be trimmed to match the surrounding pile height. Do not cut below the pile surface: short tails will pull free.
Step 6: Bind the edges
Cut rug
binding tape

into four lengths: two at 64 cm (for the short ends) and two at 94 cm (for the long sides). The extra 2 cm on each length allows for the corner mitre. Fold the tape in half lengthwise so it wraps the hessian edge with half the tape on the pile face and half on the back. Pin the tape along one long side first. At each corner, fold a 45-degree mitre pleat on the back face and pin firmly. Sew through all layers with strong linen thread and a blunt needle, using a close running stitch along the inner edge of the tape. Sew all four sides, mitre all four corners, and stitch the join at the starting point.
Step 7: Block the finished rug

Lay the finished rug pile-face down on a clean floor. Dampen the back of the hessian lightly with a damp cloth. Pull the corners square and pin them to the floor (or weight them with heavy books). Allow to dry flat, which sets the backing into shape. A rug that has dried under tension will lie noticeably flatter than one left to dry curled on a chair.
Common mistakes

items.problem: Loops pull free from the backing when the rug is walked on
items.cause: The strips were not pulled up firmly on each hook stroke, leaving the loop loose in the hessian hole. This is most common in the first few rows of a session before the tension steadies.
items.fix: After every 20 to 30 loops, press the pile from the back with a finger and check that no loops come free with light pressure. Re-hook any loose loops with a slightly firmer pull. Ensure the strip width is consistent: a strip that tapers to 12 mm or less will not grip the weave reliably.

items.problem: The rug bows upward or cups at the edges
items.cause: The hessian was not stretched taut enough on the frame before work began, or the tension of the pile rows was uneven across the width.
items.fix: Block the finished rug as described in Step 7. For severe bowing, dampen the back more thoroughly, stretch firmly, and weight or pin the full perimeter while drying. If the bow recurs with use, re-block.

items.problem: The pile surface shows uneven height across colour-change rows
items.cause: Strip width varied between colours, or the hook depth was not held consistently.
items.fix: Cut all strips to the same width before starting. Hold the hook at the same angle and depth on every stroke. At colour-change joins, bring both tails up to the same height; trim flush after completing the row.

items.problem: The binding tape lifts away from the backing at corners
items.cause: The corner mitre was not stitched through all layers, or the hessian edge was not folded firmly enough before binding.
items.fix: Open the corner mitre, refold the hessian edge more firmly, and re-sew the mitre through all layers. A second pass of stitching along the outer edge of the tape at the corner adds extra hold.

content.text: Finishing and care
Place a non-slip rug liner under the finished rug, particularly on hard floors. The hessian backing and T-shirt pile do not grip smooth surfaces well, and a sliding rug is a hazard underfoot.
Vacuum the pile regularly on a low-suction setting, running the head with the pile direction. Turning the rug over and vacuuming the back dislodges grit that surface vacuuming misses.

For spot cleaning, blot immediately with a clean absorbent cloth. Do not rub: rubbing works the stain into the pile fibres and can distort the loops. Spot-clean with cool water and a small amount of wool wash, blotting and rinsing without soaking the backing. Allow to dry flat. Do not machine-wash: the agitation loosens the pile from the backing and distorts the hessian. The binding tape may also detach under machine washing.
